title: Setting up a field water supply for livestock
subtitle: Getting reliable clean water to remote paddocks.
excerpt: Carrying water to remote paddocks in buckets is not a long-term solution for any livestock operation. A piped supply to a trough or ballcock tank is the baseline infrastructure every small holding needs before running animals in a field more than 50 metres from the house. The options range from a simple hose run to a buried supply pipe with a float-valve trough.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for Sheep Welfare (2023), water access requirements. Farming Advisory Service guidance on livestock water provision. SAC Consulting Scotland, small farm infrastructure.

glossaryTerms.definition: A mechanical valve that opens to admit water when the water level in a trough drops below a set point and closes when the water rises to the set level. Maintains a constant water level without manual filling. Standard fitting for field troughs on a mains or gravity supply.

content.text: UK welfare codes require that all livestock have clean, fresh water at all times. For field animals, that means a supply that fills itself, does not freeze in the first sharp frost, and does not grow algae.
A 50-metre garden hose from the house to a trough in the field works for a temporary or first-season setup. Connect a

to the trough inlet; the trough then fills and maintains level automatically without needing the hose attended. Disadvantages: surface hoses get damaged by animal hooves and farm machinery, freeze in winter, and restrict where you can run electric fencing across them. Suitable for seasonal use only.

Bury a 25 mm MDPE blue supply pipe at 600 mm depth (below the frost line for most of the UK lowlands) from the mains supply or a header tank to the field trough. The pipe run needs a stop-cock at both ends, at the house for shutting off over a prolonged frost, and at the trough for isolation when servicing the valve. A buried supply is the long-term standard for any permanent field; the one-off installation cost repays itself rapidly in eliminated bucket-carrying labour.

Site the trough on a well-drained hard standing or a concrete pad to prevent the area around it becoming poached mud in wet weather. One 90-litre trough per 30 sheep or 20 goats in temperate conditions; increase in hot weather or lactation. Position away from the gate and field corner to reduce crowding pressure and dominant-animal guarding of the water source.

A buried pipe is frost-protected below 600 mm. The trough will ice over in a hard frost. Use a rubber float or a trough heater to keep the water open. Hay bales around the trough give a few degrees of protection without electricity. Check water every morning in freezing weather and break ice if needed.
